sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

My flying season is going from bad to worse.  I’m currently awaiting an MRI scan (doctor’s strike allowing) on a possible hairline fracture in my foot.  To add insult to injury (sic), since I can’t walk very far, I’ve been getting out on my bicycle for exercise and fresh air.  On my first outing I did a ‘comedy’ low speed fall whilst negotiating a chicane.  My foot stuck in its toe clip.  I landed hard on a rock and really thought that I’d mashed my knee.  Mercifully that seems to be slowly getting better, but I haven’t flown for over 2 months.  There; that makes you feel more current, doesn’t it?

More positive news from our recent committee meeting.  Mike Barker has agreed to stand to replace Stef Sykes as Social Secretary at the AGM in December.  Jordan Whittaker has volunteered to replace Mike as Club Librarian.  That should bring the average age of the committee down significantly!  Trevor Birkbeck remains as willing as ever, but hang glider pilots should note that if they want to have a representative on the committee, one of them will have to stand at some stage.

Finding a volunteer to replace yours truly as Chairperson has proved more problematic.  The response to my ‘call to arms’ in the April edition of Skywords was underwhelming.  This is of particular concern since we have to provide the BHPA with the name of our chairperson, and without one we face the prospect of dissolving the club.  I realise only now that 16 years as Chairman is far too long.  Human beings get comfortable with the status quo, and change becomes a daunting challenge.  Nobody is irreplaceable and you all have a role to play: paying your membership fee and then expecting a service just doesn’t wash.    

Fortunately, Mark Morrison (Tam) has agreed to grasp the nettle and stand as chairman at the AGM, with 2 conditions.  Firstly, and most obviously, we need somebody to replace him as Secretary.  It’s really difficult to chair a meeting and take notes at the same time.  Secondly, he’ll only do the job for a year which means that we’ll be looking for another chairperson in December 2026.  It’s up to the membership how we proceed.  If one of you volunteers to be club secretary then the club will have the required officers for 2025/6. If someone is prepared to stand as chairperson, it’s likely you’ll have an experienced club secretary to assist you for a longer term.  Your call.

Perhaps we should offer free lifetime membership to retiring chairmen as an incentive 😉

In other news Stef will shortly be inviting committee members and club coaches to apply for free tickets for the Farmers’ Dinner (date changed to 18 Oct 25).  We need to encourage more pilots to attend to balance the numbers and act as hosts, and rewarding coaches seemed the best approach to take.

Take up for the FlyIn and the X-Dales has been slow so far.  We’ve realised that because the tickets are so cheap, there isn’t much incentive to buy the discounted early bird tickets.  No doubt the majority will make their decision based upon the short-term weather forecast.  The FlyIn will happen whatever the weather/numbers.  The XDales is in doubt, see below.

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

We’ve got August & September with usable weather (aside from Storm Floris which may have been fun for all you speed wing freaks out there). That’s plenty of opportunity to get your pilot tasks in ahead of going for your Pilot rating exam in the autumn. For tasks that need witnessing, here’s your handy list of coaches' contact details… https://www.dhpc.org.uk/forum/viewtopic.php?t=2977 
Not all tasks need to be seen in person:

https://www.bhpa.co.uk/documents/index.php?doc=Pilot_Tasks.pdf 

Tasks B1, C1, C2, C3, C7 will need to be seen in person and the rest can be done by showing a coach your tracklogs / flight record book / video. C6 to D2 get signed off having met a coach who’s seen your flying and talked to you about safe flying & glider recovery.  

And now for something that is not particularly coaching related, or maybe it is…


This one is more for the 2 liners and maybe 2.5 liners out there. Your speed bar is similar to a compound pulley. Mine shortens the A riser by 15cm and I push the bar 30cm which has a mechanical advantage of 2. I halve the force of pulling my A lines down or myself up, depending on your frame of reference. If me and clothes plus harness is ~100kg that means I push the bar with what feels like 50kg. It’s probably less than that since some weight will transfer to the Bs.

So far so good, we’ve pushed the bar out. Now, at the same time as the bar goes out, pull on the B toggles to negate the effects of pushing out the bar. I.e. shorten the B risers by the same amount. We’ve now just raised ourselves by 15cm using one leg and both arms. Let’s say we’ve leg pushed 50kgs and each arm has pulled 25kgs. What is the point of all this in flight callisthenics though?

Well on a glide downwind your speed doesn’t matter too much so you can play about raising yourself up and down without much negative consequence for your flight. Three sets of ten reps on each leg - that’s the time it takes for a few kilometers over the ground. It’s exercise we can take for free and get the benefits of; start the heart pumping which is going to fight the cold in spring, keep your legs active so you’re always ready to land, learn fine control of the Bs when coming on and off bar.

I’ll be adding this to my list of stuff to do each time I go on glide (when I’m back on a 2 liner). Sip of water, navigation, check for my next climb and some sets and reps for a few kilometers.

sections.title: Social News
sections.content:
Stef Sykes, Social Secretary (retiring) reports

Whilst we’re hearing reports of such amazing achievement in the air this summer, these must inevitably lead to a rich set of stories from talented flyers around the winter fire at the Horse and Farrier. Tellers of these tales are being rounded up as I write…plenty to look forward to.

However, much before then, we have some tidings of larks and lift in Settle around the first weekend in September (5th to the 7th). The fifth annual DALES FLY IN will take place on our favourite field with the good weather already booked, as  are all your late summer favourites (like ax-throwing, ahem). All your favourites will be there, to include the Ginger Nomad with his marquee of marvels, the never-ending gazebo and, of course, the unquenchable fire. Remember, you can arrive as early as Wednesday, should you wish to extend your stay due to the (planned) good weather…

To book your place, do go to the event brite page HERE.

In addition, we have the now celebrated X DALES Hike and Fly challenge set by Ed Cleasby, supported by Rob Urselmann. Details HERE.

Finally, hold the 18th of October for our Annual Flyers and Farmers Dinner. Some exciting offers for early bird bookers will follow on socials as soon as tied down this week.

Chris at the Knights Table in Stainforth will be hosting an evening of superb nosh (it really is good) with a presentation of some of our achievements in the air on a huge screen. We also enjoy an end-of-season talk from our wonderful Chair.

This is the dinner where partners are welcome, as most farmers also bring theirs!

sections.title: XDales at risk: Enter by 11-Aug !!
sections.content:
Ed Cleasby reports

This  year's XDales (details HERE, sign-up at the FLY-IN Eventbrite page HERE) is in doubt. With only four weeks to go, entries  are in low single figures; too low to make it viable. In previous  years we were actually oversubscribed entry-wise, although we lost a fair few on the day.

I suspect a lack of promotion is behind the drop off, coupled with other factors like a proliferation of ad- hoc events impacting what is usually the final h&f event of the year. Additionally, there are foreign comps we clash with.

Rob and l had a chat about the options and decided the following.

1. There will be a final call for entries extending until midnight on the 11th August (Sunday midnight)

2. If entries remain too low the XDales will be cancelled for 2025

3. If modest numbers then we'll rethink the three challenges and consider rolling them into two ..or even one.

4. If cancelled there will be a refund arranged to those entered.

With only a month to go there are things to be done which incur costs and time.. Rob is also away for the week before and l'm under a new work contract This has an impact on the jobs list/time needed.

To conclude, if the interest isn't there in sufficient numbers we cancel and that decision needs to be sooner rather than later.

As reminder, the full XDales plan is/was

  • A - RACE.  This is designed for the more experienced, fittest, equipped  and competitive. The task will be longer and more demanding.  The route will be set at short notice (24hrs)
  • B - RACE.  A shorter route, but still challenging, for fairly experienced competitors who may not have the lightest gear, but regard themselves as having a good level of fitness. As above - route set at short notice.
  • C - WAYPOINT BAGGING.  The tagging and collection of the maxiumum number of waypoints (of varying value) within an area closer to base.  A fair level of fitness required, designed for more novice H&F competitors, but able to plan their own route.  The emphasis is on a less demanding, more fun a day out.

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports on the Glorious Twelfth

The Glorious Twelfth refers to 12 August, the start of the grouse shooting season.  It is one of the busiest days in the shooting season, which for grouse, lasts until early December.  

Grouse shooting is big business - providing a significant income for landowners, and supporting the livelihoods of many people who live and work in the Dales. It also probably represents the greatest threat to restrictions on our flying sites.  We may not consider it to be particularly 'glorious' because it imposes seasonal limitations on our flying sites.  However, we share the countryside and it is vital that we respect the wishes of others.  Pen Hill has been denied to us because of grouse; and Stags Fell and Nappa Scar could very easily go the same way if we don’t get it right.

The partridge and pheasant seasons (which start in September and October respectively, and run to the end of January) are less likely to interact with flying, but we need to remain mindful of any shooting activity at flying sites.

Please note the restrictions shown on the Home and Sites pages.  We don't want to lose a site (or a pilot!).

Nappa Scar and Stags Fell - No XC flights between 12 Aug - 31 Dec.
Kilnsley - Site closed from 1st August to 31st December. No flying during this period.
Addleborough - No flying during the months of Sep - Nov unless prior approval obtained.
Wether Fell – Shoots happen at various times in autumn and winter, usually on a Saturday.  If we get the dates we'll let you know.  It's the area NE of take-off, along the cam high road.  If you see the obvious signs of a shoot stay to the SW and you should be OK.

Tom Shuttleworth (Northern Dales Sites Officer) with an update on Tailbridge

Summary: complaint received about DHPC activity on Tailbridge.  Hopefully resolved for now, but we must remain respectful and vigilant, to prevent the potential loss of access to one of our most versatile and well-flown sites.  As always, check the site guide, and encourage everyone else to do so before flying.

On the 10th July I received an email (with photographs) from a representative of the Nateby Commoners.  The complaint was that club members had parked on the ridge of Tailbridge, causing ‘yet more damage to this sensitive site’ and with a reminder that the area is a Site of Special Scientific Interest (SSSI).  The writer wanted to know ‘what the club is going to do to resolve this matter’.   Tailbridge isn’t wholly straightforward because many members of the public also park off the road on the flattish bit of grass/limestone, but some pilots (and members of the public) had indeed driven right to the T/O area.  

Site Officers receive such messages quite regularly, and I’m [almost] always encouraged by the club’s response, and this time was no different.  So here’s the good stuff 1) the person making the complaint knew exactly where to go (to send the email to me) 2) different chat groups meant that a notice could be posted fairly soon, reaching a good spread of members 3) tracking apps make it relatively easy to give a decent indication who might have been there at the time 4) I received a couple of really detailed and helpful reports (and confessions..) about what had happened - huge thanks to those who got in touch 5) the Site Guide could be updated almost immediately.  Thanks also to Martin who contacted a number of people who were at Tailbridge on the day.

I’ve let the person who wrote the complaint know what steps we’ve taken to reduce the likelihood of this happening in future, and will continue to offer the chance to speak/meet with them to discuss things in person where this is helpful.  

Hopefully, there will be no longer term consequences, but this is a reminder of how easily we risk losing access to sites where we don’t follow the guidelines.  The advice remains consistent: please continue to check the site guides before flying, and encourage others to do so.

Important announcement from North Yorks Sailwings Club (NYSC) – Sites Closure Threat

Phil Mackereth - Secretary of the NYSC committee.

Model Ridge, Carlton Bank and Cringle Moor, the main sites of the North Yorks Sailwing Club, were recently closed for flying following withdrawal by the landowner (Urra Estates) of permission to use their land, due to recurring issues. This was very disappointing for the club, as the same grievances that led to the threat of closure 5 years ago (and a subsequent Nov 2020 Skywings article informing pilots of the agreed rules), have once again led to this predicament.

The club has been in negotiations with the landowner over recent months and it is with great relief that Model Ridge, Carlton Bank and Cringle Moor have been secured again for flying. However, they do remain EXTREMELY sensitive. All pilots should adhere strictly to the site rules listed below. Any infringement perceived by the landowner could result in the permanent loss of these very valuable sites.

  • All 3 sites (Model Ridge, Carlton Bank and Cringle Bowl) are now strictly NYSC Full Members Only. Anyone wishing to fly must join the club prior to arriving at any of these sites. Membership can be obtained via the website https://membermojo.co.uk/nysc (Membership is on a yearly rolling basis from date of joining) After registering on the website, payment is by Bank Transfer or Standing Order.
  • All are asked to use the Lordstones cafe and shop whenever possible - the visibility of pilots using the Lordstones Cafe helps ensure the owners feel they are getting some return for the parking and access to take offs we all enjoy while flying these 3 sites.
  • It goes without saying that all pilots should be polite, courteous and patient with the Lordstones staff, which has not always been the case in some recent instances and will not be tolerated in the future.
  • Please be considerate with parking and only park in designated parking areas. Much of the narrow public road outside of the Lord Stones entrance is double yellow lined and the verges are owned by the landowner. Parking on verges is not tolerated and many parking tickets have previously been issued.
  • If the gate to on-site additional parking is closed and the normal car park is full, we can request that the overflow car parking be opened. If the staff are busy however, please be patient - the customers in the cafe are their first priority, not pilots wanting to get in the air.

The North Yorks Sailwing Club is also proud to announce that, as part of the agreement with the landowner, the club has organised and paid for the purchase, installation and ongoing maintenance of a public defibrillator at the Lordstones Cafe site.  This has been installed and was brought into service in July. It is located on the Amenities block right by the popular Coast to Coast and Cleveland Way footpath. It is in a code locked cabinet, is fully automatic and gives full audible instructions for use. The unlock code will be given by the 999 Operator on calling an incident in. Huge thanks again go to those involved in the speedy installation of this unit.

The NYSC wish to urge all pilots to abide by the above site rules and thank you all in advance for doing so. We only have access to these sites due to the generosity of the landowners and we need to respect them and their wishes or we will lose these sites.

sections.title: First Aid
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann - First Aid Officer reports

Do YOU know what to do if you see your mate have a ‘bad landing’ ?

Having had a fully booked and very well received first aid course in November last year, I’m now canvassing if there is interest in another course this year.

The idea is to do similar to last year with just a one-day course, hopefully in similar or same central location, and with costs also similar (last year was £65 pp, but ended up with a £20pp rebate being fully booked).

Having been in touch with Allan Shaw again, we're looking at w/e of 29 Nov or 6 Dec.

If you are interested, please let me know via firstaid@dhpc.org.uk, WA or Telegram including preferred weekend if required (either way i’ll need your email and phone details).

If there is enough interest, the next step will be to find an instructor (hopefully Alan again), date and venue. Only then will you be required to pay up to confirm your spot.

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Clive Sury reports

Flying solo

 

 

A few weeks ago, I was at Windbank on my own.  It was late afternoon, with just 2 other cars in the bottom field and I passed what I perceived to be the last walker of the day on their way down as I was on my way up.  Whilst it was a delightfully peaceful environment, before taking off, it occurred to me that to mitigate the risk of flying solo, it would be prudent to let someone else know my whereabouts.  Noting that there have been numerous instances where people have flown solo, then had an incident and with detrimental outcomes compounded by the time it’s taken for them to be found.

What occurred to me as a simple precaution to do was to inform a chat group that I was about to fly, asking them to keep a look out for me to check in again post flight that evening.  As a minimum this would mean that I had a backstop.  The conditions could be described as benign and Windbank is a well-known site to me with lots of options to land and not too far from a road.  However, it was evident that it was increasingly isolated with little chance of passing traffic or others around should I need help.  Once I contacted the group, acknowledgement replies came back very quickly, and it’s yet another example of how supportive others are in this sport.

Post flight I checked back in and was again acknowledged by the people acting as lookouts.  Upon reflection though I realised that by posting to a group, I may have unnecessarily asked too many people to act as a backup by this approach.  Therefore, after a short discussion, what has been agreed is that if a person posts a request for lookout, then the first 1 or 2 people confirming with a thumbs up act as guardians.  In this way avoiding anyone assuming that someone else is fulfilling this role, plus avoiding duplication.

I mention this to raise awareness of the risk of flying solo.  Best practice is to always inform someone of where you’re flying, what your planned flight route is and likely timings. Then check back in post flight.  Also it’s worth reiterating from Pete Logan’s recent article that there are many areas in the Dales without phone/ data coverage and therefore, it is advisable to let someone know your flying intentions before going to a site.

More adventurous pilots going XC or in remote terrain use personal trackers, such as Garmin Inreach and there’s also mobile apps such as Google/ Apple livetracking or Volandoo.com.  Plus the iPhone 14 and later models have a satellite emergency SOS service.  Also, many varios have trackers that link to Puretrack.  All of which are recommended for tracking pilots for safety.  However, the purpose of this article is to raise awareness of solo flying that’s done when people may not be on XC’s or out in the wilds but at one of our local sites where risks of not being found are still present, and this can be mitigated against.

Incident Nont Sarahs

Earlier this year there was an incident at Nont Sarahs involving a paraglider colliding with a model aircraft.  This note is to remind everyone to read the site guide before flying at this (and every) site.  In this case, please pay particular attention to the respective flying areas between model aircraft and free flyers.  Plus, on any site it is advisable to talk with model aircraft flyers beforehand, and not to assume someone else has.

Nont Sarahs | Pennine Soaring Club

Safe flying,

Clive.

sections.title: Krusevo British & Dutch Open
sections.content:
Pete Logan reports


We were often befriended by locals… maybe because we saved some of the fine meat products for them.

Dave Smart, Chris Fountain & Richard Butty. Part of the northern contingent. Also there in our group were Rahul Basu, Richard Meek, Jacob Butterworth & Martin Underdown.  

And here’s Task 6, just because it’s a nice shape.
